items.q: Where is almost all the mass of an atom located?
items.correct: In the nucleus, made up of protons and neutrons.
items.misconception: Electrons contribute significantly to the mass of an atom.
items.random_wrong: Mass is spread evenly throughout the entire atom including the electron shells.
items.why: Students see electrons listed alongside protons and neutrons and assume they contribute comparable mass — in reality an electron has ~1/1836 the mass of a proton.

items.q: What is different between two isotopes of the same element?
items.correct: They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
items.misconception: Isotopes have different numbers of electrons, which is why their mass differs.
items.random_wrong: Isotopes are atoms of different elements with the same mass.
items.why: Students conflate isotopes with ions because both alter a property of the atom — but ions change electrons (charge), isotopes change neutrons (mass).

items.q: Why is the 4s orbital filled before the 3d orbital?
items.correct: Because the 4s orbital has a lower energy than 3d when both are empty, so electrons fill it first.
items.misconception: Because the 4s shell is closer to the nucleus than the 3d shell.
items.random_wrong: Because d orbitals can only fill after p orbitals in the next shell.
items.why: Students assume shell number directly correlates with distance/energy ordering, so 3-anything must come before 4-anything — but energy ordering crosses over at 4s/3d.

items.q: What happens to electrons when sodium reacts with chlorine?
items.correct: Sodium loses one electron to chlorine; the resulting Na+ and Cl- ions attract electrostatically.
items.misconception: Sodium and chlorine share an electron between them.
items.random_wrong: Protons move from chlorine to sodium to balance the charges.
items.why: Students over-apply the covalent (sharing) model to all bonding because sharing is introduced first and feels intuitive.

items.q: When water boils, what is broken?
items.correct: Intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonds) between water molecules are broken — the O-H covalent bonds stay intact.
items.misconception: The covalent bonds between hydrogen and oxygen atoms are broken when water boils.
items.random_wrong: Water molecules split into hydrogen and oxygen gas when they boil.
items.why: Students don't distinguish between bonds within a molecule and forces between molecules, so they assume boiling 'breaks the molecule apart'.

items.q: Why do metals conduct electricity?
items.correct: Delocalised electrons are free to move through the lattice of positive ions, carrying charge.
items.misconception: Metal atoms themselves move to carry the current.
items.random_wrong: Metals contain naturally moving protons that act as charge carriers.
items.why: Students picture conduction as 'something moving through the wire' and default to whole atoms, since they don't fully grasp delocalisation.

items.q: In an exothermic reaction, what happens energetically with the bonds?
items.correct: Energy released forming new bonds exceeds energy absorbed breaking existing bonds.
items.misconception: Bond breaking releases energy and bond making absorbs energy.
items.random_wrong: The reactants get colder because they lose mass as energy.
items.why: Students confuse the direction: they think breaking is 'releasing' (like cracking something open releases contents), when actually breaking always requires energy input.

items.q: How does a catalyst speed up a reaction?
items.correct: It provides an alternative reaction pathway with a lower activation energy.
items.misconception: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of the original reaction pathway.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst gives extra kinetic energy to the reactant particles.
items.why: Textbook diagrams often show 'Ea lowered' without making clear it's a new pathway, leading students to think catalysts modify the original pathway.

items.q: Why does increasing temperature increase the rate of reaction significantly?
items.correct: Mainly because a much larger fraction of collisions have energy at least equal to the activation energy; collision frequency increases only slightly.
items.misconception: Particles collide much more frequently at higher temperature, which is the main reason for the rate increase.
items.random_wrong: Higher temperature increases the concentration of the reactants.
items.why: Students focus on the more intuitive 'faster movement = more collisions' and overlook that the exponential effect comes from the energy distribution shifting past Ea.

items.q: Does a catalyst affect the position of equilibrium?
items.correct: No — it speeds up both the forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium is reached faster but at the same position.
items.misconception: A catalyst shifts the equilibrium towards the products.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst increases the yield by being consumed in the forward reaction only.
items.why: Students associate 'catalyst' with 'helps the reaction' and assume that means 'pushes towards products' rather than 'speeds up both directions equally'.

items.q: At dynamic equilibrium, what is happening to the reactions?
items.correct: Forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates; concentrations remain constant.
items.misconception: Both reactions stop because the system has reached balance.
items.random_wrong: The forward reaction stops once the reverse reaction begins.
items.why: The word 'equilibrium' connotes stillness, so students think reactions stop — they miss that equilibrium is dynamic at the particle level.

items.q: What is reduction in terms of electrons?
items.correct: Gain of electrons (the oxidation number is reduced).
items.misconception: Reduction means losing oxygen and oxidation means gaining oxygen — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Reduction means a substance becomes physically smaller in size.
items.why: Students learn the oxygen definition first (from rusting/combustion) and never update to the more general electron-transfer definition.

items.q: How do alkenes typically react with bromine?
items.correct: By electrophilic addition — bromine adds across the double bond to form a dibromoalkane.
items.misconception: Bromine substitutes for a hydrogen on the alkene, releasing HBr.
items.random_wrong: The alkene polymerises with bromine to form a long chain.
items.why: Students see 'reaction with halogen' and default to the substitution they learned for alkanes, not recognising that the C=C changes the mechanism.

items.q: What is the product of mild oxidation of a primary alcohol?
items.correct: An aldehyde (which can be further oxidised to a carboxylic acid).
items.misconception: A primary alcohol is oxidised directly to a ketone.
items.random_wrong: A primary alcohol is oxidised to an alkene by losing water.
items.why: Students confuse the products of primary vs secondary alcohol oxidation because they look structurally similar, but only secondary alcohols form ketones.

items.q: What is the difference between a strong acid and a concentrated acid?
items.correct: A strong acid fully dissociates in water; a concentrated acid has a high amount of acid per volume — these are independent properties.
items.misconception: Strong and concentrated mean the same thing for acids.
items.random_wrong: A strong acid is one that has been heated to higher temperature.
items.why: In everyday language 'strong' and 'concentrated' are synonyms (e.g. strong coffee), so students transfer that meaning to chemistry.

items.q: When an acid neutralises an alkali, what is the pH of the resulting solution?
items.correct: Approximately 7 if equivalent amounts of strong acid and strong alkali react — but it varies with strength.
items.misconception: Neutralisation always produces a solution with pH exactly 7 regardless of the acid and base used.
items.random_wrong: Neutralisation always produces a solution with pH 0.
items.why: Students learn the rule 'neutral = 7' so rigidly that they ignore weak-acid/weak-base cases where the salt is acidic or basic.

items.q: Why does first ionisation energy generally increase across a period?
items.correct: Nuclear charge increases while shielding stays roughly constant, so electrons are held more tightly.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger across a period, making electrons easier to add but harder to remove.
items.random_wrong: Atoms across a period have more shells, pulling electrons closer.
items.why: Students confuse the direction of the atomic-radius trend across a period (it actually decreases) and use wrong size reasoning.

items.q: At which electrode are positive ions reduced during electrolysis?
items.correct: At the cathode (the negative electrode) — positive ions gain electrons there.
items.misconception: Positive ions are reduced at the anode because that's where positive things go.
items.random_wrong: Positive ions are oxidised at the cathode by losing protons.
items.why: Students associate 'positive' with 'attracted to positive' or get muddled by the sign conventions and switch the electrodes.

items.q: What does a mole of a substance represent?
items.correct: Avogadro's number (6.02 x 10^23) of particles of that substance.
items.misconception: A mole is a unit of mass equal to the relative molecular mass in grams of any substance.
items.random_wrong: A mole is a unit of volume equal to 24 dm^3 at any temperature.
items.why: Students conflate the mole (a count) with molar mass (a mass-per-mole), because in practice they use moles to convert to grams.

items.q: At room temperature and pressure, what volume does 1 mole of any gas occupy?
items.correct: Approximately 24 dm^3 (24,000 cm^3), regardless of the identity of the gas.
items.misconception: Heavier gases occupy a smaller volume per mole because their particles are denser.
items.random_wrong: Different gases occupy completely different volumes proportional to their molar mass.
items.why: Students assume heavier particles must be more compact, missing that gas volume depends only on number of particles, T and P (Avogadro's law).

items.q: What does an increase in entropy of a system correspond to?
items.correct: An increase in the number of ways energy and particles can be arranged — typically a more dispersed/'disordered' state.
items.misconception: Entropy is just a measure of how messy a system looks visually.
items.random_wrong: Entropy measures how much energy a system contains in total.
items.why: The 'disorder' analogy is overused: students take it literally (mess vs tidy) instead of as a count of microstates.

items.q: If a reaction has a negative Gibbs free energy change, what does that mean?
items.correct: The reaction is thermodynamically feasible — it can proceed — but it may still be very slow.
items.misconception: A reaction with negative delta G will always happen quickly.
items.random_wrong: A reaction with negative delta G must be exothermic in every case.
items.why: Students conflate thermodynamic feasibility with kinetics, assuming 'spontaneous' means 'fast' rather than 'energetically allowed'.

items.q: What is a hydrogen bond?
items.correct: A strong dipole-dipole interaction between an H atom bonded to N/O/F and a lone pair on another electronegative atom.
items.misconception: A hydrogen bond is a covalent bond involving hydrogen.
items.random_wrong: A hydrogen bond is a bond formed when hydrogen molecules fuse together.
items.why: The word 'bond' makes students think of intramolecular bonding; they don't realise H-bonds are intermolecular forces, much weaker than covalent bonds.

items.q: What distinguishes structural isomers from stereoisomers?
items.correct: Structural isomers have different atom connectivity; stereoisomers have the same connectivity but different spatial arrangement.
items.misconception: Stereoisomers are just structural isomers drawn from a different angle.
items.random_wrong: Stereoisomers have different numbers of atoms but the same molecular formula.
items.why: Students don't always grasp that 3D arrangement is chemically meaningful; rotating a drawing on paper feels like the same molecule, so stereoisomerism feels artificial.

items.q: Why does benzene undergo substitution rather than addition reactions?
items.correct: Adding across a C=C would destroy the stable delocalised pi system; substitution preserves the aromatic ring.
items.misconception: Benzene contains three normal alternating double bonds and behaves like an alkene with three C=C.
items.random_wrong: Benzene cannot react at all because it's too stable.
items.why: The Kekule diagram shows fixed double bonds, so students assume benzene should behave like cyclohexatriene — missing the resonance/delocalisation that changes its reactivity.

items.q: In a standard hydrogen electrode used as a reference, what is the assigned electrode potential?
items.correct: Exactly 0 V by convention — all other electrode potentials are measured relative to it.
items.misconception: The standard hydrogen electrode actually has zero potential because hydrogen has no charge.
items.random_wrong: The standard hydrogen electrode has a potential of -1 V to balance copper at +1 V.
items.why: Students don't realise 0 V is a chosen reference, not a measured value; they think hydrogen is genuinely neutral electrochemically.

items.q: How does a buffer maintain its pH when small amounts of acid are added?
items.correct: The conjugate base in the buffer reacts with added H+ to remove it, keeping pH nearly constant.
items.misconception: A buffer prevents any H+ ions from being added to the solution at all.
items.random_wrong: A buffer destroys added acid by changing it into water vapour.
items.why: Students treat 'buffer' as a magical pH-locker rather than a chemical equilibrium that absorbs H+ or OH- through reactions with its components.

items.q: Why does Hess's Law work?
items.correct: Enthalpy is a state function — change depends only on initial and final states, not the route taken.
items.misconception: Hess's Law works because energy always cancels out around any cycle.
items.random_wrong: Hess's Law works because reactions can only happen in one specific direction.
items.why: Students learn Hess's Law as an algebra rule for adding equations and miss the underlying physical reason about state functions.

items.q: Why does water have a bent shape rather than linear?
items.correct: Oxygen has two bonding pairs and two lone pairs, giving a tetrahedral electron geometry but a bent molecular shape.
items.misconception: Water is bent because the hydrogens repel each other and push apart.
items.random_wrong: Water is bent because oxygen is heavier than hydrogen and weighs the middle down.
items.why: Students see H atoms at the ends and intuit H-H repulsion, missing that the bend is caused by lone pair-bond pair repulsion on the oxygen.

items.q: Why does atomic radius increase down a group?
items.correct: Each successive element has an additional electron shell, increasing the distance of outer electrons from the nucleus.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger down a group because they have more protons attracting more matter.
items.random_wrong: Atoms get bigger down a group because nuclei swell when they have more neutrons.
items.why: Students attribute size to mass/protons (a 'more stuff = bigger' intuition) rather than to the number of shells.

items.q: What does a curly arrow in an organic mechanism represent?
items.correct: The movement of a pair of electrons from where they are to where they end up.
items.misconception: A curly arrow shows the movement of an atom from one position to another.
items.random_wrong: A curly arrow shows the direction in which heat is flowing in the reaction.
items.why: Curly arrows look like they're pointing to atoms, so students think they describe atomic motion rather than electron pair movement.

items.q: If 50 cm^3 of a 1 mol/dm^3 solution is diluted to 100 cm^3, what is the new concentration?
items.correct: 0.5 mol/dm^3 — the moles stay the same but the volume doubled.
items.misconception: The new concentration is 0.05 mol/dm^3 because you doubled the volume so you halve the concentration once and shift the decimal too.
items.random_wrong: The new concentration is 2 mol/dm^3 because adding water makes the solution stronger.
items.why: Students panic with unit conversions and over-divide, or they confuse 'add water = more concentrated' through a misread of the procedure.

items.q: Why does graphite conduct electricity but diamond does not?
items.correct: Graphite has delocalised electrons between layers; diamond has all four outer electrons in localised covalent bonds.
items.misconception: Graphite conducts because it is a metal, while diamond is a non-metal.
items.random_wrong: Graphite conducts because it can melt easily at room temperature.
items.why: Students associate 'conducts electricity' with 'is a metal' and don't recognise that some non-metallic allotropes have free electrons too.

items.q: Is the oxidation state of an atom the same as its actual charge?
items.correct: Not necessarily — oxidation state is an accounting tool assuming all bonds are fully ionic; real charge is usually smaller.
items.misconception: The oxidation state of an atom is exactly equal to its real ionic charge in every compound.
items.random_wrong: The oxidation state of an atom is always equal to the number of electrons it has.
items.why: Oxidation states are written with charge-like signs, so students treat them as actual charges, particularly in covalent compounds where this is misleading.

items.q: Can you determine the order of a reaction with respect to a reactant from the balanced equation?
items.correct: No — orders must be determined experimentally; they often differ from the stoichiometric coefficients.
items.misconception: The order of reaction with respect to each reactant equals its coefficient in the balanced equation.
items.random_wrong: The order of reaction is always equal to the number of reactants in the equation.
items.why: Students see numbers in the balanced equation and try to reuse them — but rate laws depend on the mechanism, not just stoichiometry.

items.q: Why are most transition metal complex ions coloured?
items.correct: Ligands split the d-orbitals into two energy levels; electrons absorb visible light to jump between them (d-d transitions), and the complementary colour is transmitted.
items.misconception: Transition metal ions are coloured because they have a half-full d-subshell which naturally absorbs light.
items.random_wrong: Transition metals are coloured because their nuclei emit photons in the visible range.
items.why: Students see 'd-block = coloured' as a label but miss that colour requires a partially filled d-subshell AND ligand-induced splitting — Sc3+ and Zn2+ are colourless because the d level is empty or full.

items.q: Why can transition metals show variable oxidation states?
items.correct: The 4s and 3d orbitals are similar in energy, so different numbers of electrons can be removed without a large energy cost.
items.misconception: Transition metals have variable oxidation states because they have many shells of electrons.
items.random_wrong: Transition metals have variable oxidation states because they can gain protons easily.
items.why: Students attribute it vaguely to 'lots of electrons' rather than the specific 4s/3d energy proximity that makes successive ionisations comparable in cost.

items.q: What kind of bond forms between a ligand and a central metal ion in a complex?
items.correct: A dative (coordinate) covalent bond — the ligand donates a lone pair into an empty orbital on the metal.
items.misconception: The ligand forms an ionic bond with the metal because the metal is positively charged.
items.random_wrong: The ligand shares electrons equally with the metal as a normal covalent bond.
items.why: Students see the +ve metal and assume electrostatic ionic bonding, missing that ligands donate lone pairs into vacant d-orbitals.

items.q: If a complex ion has a coordination number of 4, what shape does it have?
items.correct: It can be either tetrahedral OR square planar — the shape depends on the metal and ligands (e.g. [Pt(NH3)4]2+ is square planar; [CuCl4]2- is tetrahedral).
items.misconception: A coordination number of 4 always means tetrahedral, because that gives the maximum bond-angle separation.
items.random_wrong: A coordination number of 4 always means square planar because complexes prefer to be flat.
items.why: Students apply VSEPR-style reasoning and default to tetrahedral, missing that d8 metals like Pt(II) form square planar complexes for electronic reasons.

items.q: What distinguishes a heterogeneous from a homogeneous catalyst?
items.correct: A heterogeneous catalyst is in a different phase from the reactants; a homogeneous catalyst is in the same phase.
items.misconception: Heterogeneous catalysts are solid and homogeneous catalysts are liquid — full stop.
items.random_wrong: Heterogeneous catalysts are consumed; homogeneous catalysts are not.
items.why: Students learn 'solid catalyst' examples (iron in Haber) and over-generalise — the actual distinction is about phase relative to the reactants, not the catalyst's physical state alone.

items.q: What are the units of Kc for a reaction?
items.correct: They depend on Δn (the difference between moles of gaseous/aqueous products and reactants) — Kc can be dimensionless or have units like mol dm^-3.
items.misconception: Kc always has units of mol dm^-3.
items.random_wrong: Kc is always dimensionless.
items.why: Students learn Kc with a fixed unit example and don't realise units arise from the exponents in the expression; for A + B ⇌ C + D, Kc is unitless, but for A ⇌ B + C, Kc has units.

items.q: When a stress shifts an equilibrium, does the system fully undo the change?
items.correct: No — Le Chatelier's principle says the system PARTIALLY opposes the change; equilibrium shifts to a new position that minimises but doesn't eliminate the disturbance.
items.misconception: The system shifts to completely cancel the change and return concentrations to their original values.
items.random_wrong: The system shifts to amplify the change because reactions always proceed in the disturbed direction.
items.why: Students treat Le Chatelier as a perfect homeostat, missing that the new equilibrium reflects the new conditions and only partially counters the disturbance.

items.q: What does Kp use instead of concentrations?
items.correct: Partial pressures of each gaseous component at equilibrium.
items.misconception: Kp uses the total pressure of the system raised to the stoichiometric coefficients.
items.random_wrong: Kp uses the volumes of each gas in the mixture.
items.why: Students conflate total and partial pressure; they think Kp is just Kc with 'pressure' substituted, missing that each species has its own partial pressure.

items.q: Does adding a catalyst change the value of Kc?
items.correct: No — a catalyst speeds up forward and reverse reactions equally, so Kc is unchanged.
items.misconception: A catalyst increases Kc by favouring product formation.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst decreases Kc by stabilising the reactants.
items.why: Students think catalysts 'help the reaction' so they must increase Kc; missing that Kc is determined by thermodynamics (ΔG), not by rate.

items.q: Why can an endothermic reaction still be spontaneous?
items.correct: If the entropy increase (TΔS) is large enough to outweigh the positive ΔH, ΔG can still be negative.
items.misconception: Endothermic reactions are never spontaneous because they require energy input.
items.random_wrong: All endothermic reactions are spontaneous at room temperature.
items.why: Students equate 'exothermic' with 'spontaneous' from GCSE, missing the role of entropy in Gibbs free energy.

items.q: What state are the ions in when defining lattice enthalpy of formation?
items.correct: Gaseous ions — lattice enthalpy is the energy change when one mole of solid ionic compound forms from its constituent ions in the GAS phase.
items.misconception: The ions are in aqueous solution before forming the lattice.
items.random_wrong: The ions start as solid elements before being assembled into the lattice.
items.why: Students confuse lattice enthalpy with enthalpy of solution; they don't realise the Born-Haber cycle requires gas-phase ions as the reference point.

items.q: Why is the hydration enthalpy of a small, highly charged ion very exothermic?
items.correct: Small/highly charged ions form strong ion-dipole attractions with water molecules, releasing a large amount of energy.
items.misconception: Hydration enthalpy only depends on the size of the ion, not its charge.
items.random_wrong: Hydration enthalpy is the same for all ions because water molecules are identical.
items.why: Students focus on one factor (size OR charge) when actually both matter — charge density determines the strength of ion-dipole interactions.

items.q: When you reverse a reaction in a Hess cycle, what happens to ΔH?
items.correct: Its sign reverses — if the forward reaction is +100 kJ/mol, the reverse is -100 kJ/mol.
items.misconception: ΔH stays the same because it's the same reaction.
items.random_wrong: ΔH becomes zero because the change cancels out.
items.why: Students treat ΔH as a property of the reactants/products rather than a directed quantity that flips with direction.

items.q: Why do tertiary halogenoalkanes typically react via SN1 rather than SN2?
items.correct: The tertiary carbocation intermediate is stabilised by three alkyl groups (positive inductive effect), making the SN1 pathway favourable.
items.misconception: Tertiary halogenoalkanes react via SN1 because they are more reactive in every way.
items.random_wrong: Tertiary halogenoalkanes react via SN2 because they have more bonds to break.
items.why: Students mix up steric and electronic factors — they don't realise it's the STABILITY of the carbocation (electronic) plus STERIC HINDRANCE (blocking SN2) that drives the SN1 preference.

items.q: Why does benzene undergo electrophilic SUBSTITUTION but alkenes undergo electrophilic ADDITION?
items.correct: Substitution preserves benzene's stable delocalised pi system; addition would destroy aromaticity at a large energetic cost.
items.misconception: Benzene reacts the same way as alkenes — by addition across one of its double bonds.
items.random_wrong: Benzene cannot react with electrophiles at all.
items.why: Students see C=C in both structures and expect identical mechanisms, missing that delocalisation fundamentally changes the energetics.

items.q: When HBr adds to propene, why does Br preferentially attach to the middle carbon?
items.correct: The more stable secondary carbocation forms preferentially as the intermediate, so Br adds there (Markovnikov's rule).
items.misconception: Br adds to whichever carbon has more hydrogens already.
items.random_wrong: Br always adds to the end carbon because it's the most accessible.
items.why: Students memorise 'rich get richer' for hydrogens but don't connect it to carbocation stability — the rule emerges from intermediate stability, not from a stoichiometric pattern.

items.q: What is the relationship between two enantiomers?
items.correct: They are non-superimposable mirror images that rotate plane-polarised light in opposite directions but have identical chemical and physical properties otherwise.
items.misconception: Enantiomers have different boiling points and react completely differently with all reagents.
items.random_wrong: Enantiomers are the same molecule drawn differently on paper.
items.why: Students don't realise enantiomers are chemically indistinguishable except in chiral environments (light, biological receptors); they assume all isomers must behave differently.

items.q: Can a single standard electrode potential be measured in isolation?
items.correct: No — electrode potentials are RELATIVE values measured against a reference (the standard hydrogen electrode, defined as 0 V).
items.misconception: Each electrode has an absolute potential that can be measured directly with a voltmeter.
items.random_wrong: Standard electrode potentials are measured by submerging the electrode in pure water.
items.why: Students think a voltmeter can give a single electrode's value; they don't grasp that potential difference requires two electrodes by definition.

items.q: If E°cell for a redox reaction is positive, what does that tell you?
items.correct: The reaction is thermodynamically feasible — but it may still be very slow if kinetic barriers are high.
items.misconception: A positive E°cell guarantees the reaction will happen rapidly at room temperature.
items.random_wrong: A positive E°cell means the reaction is exothermic in every case.
items.why: Students equate 'feasible' with 'fast', missing the thermodynamics-vs-kinetics distinction (same error as with negative ΔG).

items.q: What is the purpose of a salt bridge in an electrochemical cell?
items.correct: It completes the circuit by allowing ions to flow between the half-cells, maintaining electrical neutrality.
items.misconception: The salt bridge carries electrons between the two electrodes.
items.random_wrong: The salt bridge prevents the solutions from reacting with each other.
items.why: Students confuse the internal circuit (ion flow through salt bridge) with the external circuit (electron flow through wire) — both are needed, but they carry different charge carriers.

items.q: How does a fuel cell differ from a conventional battery?
items.correct: A fuel cell continuously consumes externally supplied fuel (e.g. hydrogen) and oxidant; a battery has a fixed amount of reactants that get used up.
items.misconception: Fuel cells and batteries are identical — both just store electrical energy.
items.random_wrong: Fuel cells burn fuel to generate heat which is then converted to electricity.
items.why: Students think 'fuel' implies combustion; they miss that fuel cells generate electricity directly from redox reactions, continuously refuelled.

items.q: What does an NMR chemical shift (δ, in ppm) actually measure?
items.correct: The position of a peak RELATIVE to TMS (tetramethylsilane, δ = 0), normalised by the spectrometer frequency.
items.misconception: Chemical shift is the absolute resonance frequency of the nucleus in Hz.
items.random_wrong: Chemical shift is the mass of the molecule divided by 1000.
items.why: Students see ppm and think it's an absolute scale, not realising it's a frequency-independent ratio against a reference.

items.q: In 1H NMR, what does the n+1 rule predict?
items.correct: A proton with n equivalent neighbouring protons will appear as a peak split into n+1 sub-peaks.
items.misconception: Splitting tells you how many protons are in the same chemical environment as the one giving the peak.
items.random_wrong: The number of peaks equals the total number of protons in the molecule.
items.why: Students confuse splitting (about NEIGHBOURING protons) with the number of equivalent protons producing the peak (which affects peak area/integration, not splitting).

items.q: What is the role of the fingerprint region (below ~1500 cm^-1) in IR spectroscopy?
items.correct: It is unique to each compound — used to identify a specific molecule by matching to a known spectrum.
items.misconception: The fingerprint region is used to identify which functional groups are present.
items.random_wrong: The fingerprint region is the part that contains all the most useful absorptions.
items.why: Students reverse functional group region (above ~1500, identifies bond types) and fingerprint region (below, identifies the molecule as a whole) — both are useful, but for different purposes.

items.q: What does the M+ peak in a mass spectrum represent?
items.correct: The molecular ion — formed when a single electron is removed from the whole molecule, giving the relative molecular mass.
items.misconception: The M+ peak shows the mass of the largest fragment after the molecule breaks apart.
items.random_wrong: The M+ peak shows the mass of a single hydrogen atom added to the molecule.
items.why: Students confuse the M+ peak (the parent ion at the molecular mass) with fragment peaks; they think the highest mass peak is always a fragment, not the intact ionised molecule.

items.q: Why does the colour of a transition metal complex change when ligands are exchanged?
items.correct: Different ligands cause different amounts of d-orbital splitting, so different wavelengths of light are absorbed.
items.misconception: The colour changes because the metal ion itself changes oxidation state.
items.random_wrong: The colour changes because the new ligand is itself coloured.
items.why: Students assume colour comes from the ligand or from a redox change; they miss that the same metal ion gives different colours depending on which ligands are bound.

items.q: How does the rate of an SN2 reaction depend on concentrations?
items.correct: Rate is first order in both substrate AND nucleophile (overall second order) because both are involved in the rate-determining step.
items.misconception: SN2 reactions are first order overall because only the substrate matters.
items.random_wrong: SN2 reactions are zero order because they happen in one step.
items.why: Students confuse SN1 (rate depends only on substrate) and SN2 (rate depends on both); the '2' in SN2 refers to the order, but they forget which way round.

items.q: How is a dynamic equilibrium different from a static one?
items.correct: In a dynamic equilibrium, forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates; in a static equilibrium, nothing is happening at the particle level.
items.misconception: Dynamic and static equilibria are the same thing because both show constant macroscopic properties.
items.random_wrong: A dynamic equilibrium means the system is still changing visibly.
items.why: Macroscopically both look identical (no observable change), so students don't grasp the crucial particle-level distinction.

items.q: For a process to be spontaneous, what must happen to the total entropy of the universe?
items.correct: It must increase (ΔS_total > 0) — the system's entropy alone can decrease as long as the surroundings' entropy increases more.
items.misconception: The entropy of the system must increase for the process to be spontaneous.
items.random_wrong: The entropy of the surroundings must stay constant for the process to be spontaneous.
items.why: Students apply the second law only to the system, missing that what counts is system + surroundings (the whole universe) — which is why exothermic reactions can be spontaneous even with ΔS_system < 0.
